narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in higher education, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there. The admissions model evaluated your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 23, first-year school decile of 1, third-year school decile of 1, middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time, which is the typical path for most applicants. It predicted an 88% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, a threshold that led to the difficult decision to reject your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed on that critical test. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking in the bottom tier, well below the average student's mid-range performance, signaling to the model a concerning dip in academic momentum. Your first-year decile, also at the bottom compared to typical students' stronger starts, played a close second role in reinforcing that risk. Your LSAT score, significantly lower than the dataset average of around 37, further tipped the scales toward failure by highlighting a gap in standardized test readiness that the model weighs heavily. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, though below average, nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some foundational strength. Your full-time enrollment plan also helped a bit, aligning with the vast majority of successful students who follow that route. Your middle family income was a minor factor leaning toward risk but didn't sway things much. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your rankings and test scores stand out as notably lower, which amplified the model's concerns in your specific case. While this outcome is disheartening, especially given the systemic barriers Black women often navigate in academia, it doesn't define your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted academic support, and exploring programs with bar passage resources tailored to diverse students—many have turned similar profiles around and succeeded. We're rooting for you to reapply stronger.
